headline: Brian Schweitzer Sworn In as 23rd Governor of Montana, 2005
body:
On the third day of January in the year 2005, a fellow by the name of Brian Schweitzer ambled into the governor's office in Helena, taking up the reins as the twenty-third man to hold the job. Now, Schweitzer was not your usual sort of politician, all starched collars and careful words. He was a farmer from Havre, a man who knew his way around agronomy and soil science, and who had spent a good deal of time building irrigation projects in places like Libya and Saudi Arabia before deciding he ought to try his hand at running Montana. He brought with him a certain folksy charm, a border collie named Jag who had the run of the Capitol, and a habit of speaking his mind that left the more delicate sensibilities in the legislature clutching their pearls. He was a Democrat in a state that often leaned the other way, but he had a knack for talking to folks in a way that made them forget about party lines and just listen to the man.

His time in office, which stretched out until the early days of 2013, was a lively affair. He was a man who liked to use his veto pen, and he used it with a flourish. In the 2011 session alone, he struck down seventy-four bills, and not a single one of those vetoes was overridden. He even took to using a branding iron to stamp "VETO" on bills he found particularly disagreeable, calling them "frivolous, unconstitutional and just bad ideas." It was a spectacle, to be sure, but it was also a clear sign that he was not a man to be trifled with. He pushed hard for education, signing into law a full-time kindergarten program and finally putting some money behind the mandate to teach tribal history in the schools. He also oversaw a mighty increase in the number of folks getting college degrees, figuring that a smart populace was a prosperous one.

But perhaps the most telling thing about the man was his way with the Native tribes of Montana. He made a point of including them in the workings of the state, appointing more Indians to boards and commissions than all the previous governors combined. He understood that the history of the state was tied up with the history of the tribes, and he sought to mend some of the old fences. As he put it in a report on tribal relations, "It was about eight years ago that I said to you, if you will trust me, we can create a new Montana, a Montana where we respect one another." He even managed to bring the Yellowstone buffalo back to the Fort Peck and Fort Belknap reservations, a move that spoke volumes about his commitment to honoring the past while looking to the future. He left office with the state's coffers full and his approval ratings high, a farmer who had come to town and shown them all how it was done.
